Name: information_theoretic_model_compression
Title: Exploring the Fundamental Limits of Model Compression: An Information-Theoretic Approach
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fundamental limits of model compression can be characterized using information-theoretic tools, such as rate-distortion theory and the information bottleneck method. By framing model compression as a lossy source coding problem, we can derive achievable bounds on the compression ratio and distortion tradeoff, and identify the most efficient compression algorithms.
Related Work: Recent works such as EMQ, AMP-ViT, and SNIP have explored mixed-precision quantization and adaptive compression techniques for deep learning models. However, these works focus on empirical methods and do not provide a fundamental understanding of the limits of model compression. Our work differs from these approaches by taking an information-theoretic perspective, which allows us to derive theoretical bounds and guarantees on the compression performance.
Abstract: Model compression is a crucial technique for deploying deep learning models on resource-constrained devices. However, the fundamental limits of model compression are not well understood. In this work, we take an information-theoretic approach to characterize the limits of model compression. We frame model compression as a lossy source coding problem and derive achievable bounds on the compression ratio and distortion tradeoff using rate-distortion theory and the information bottleneck method. Our results provide a theoretical foundation for understanding the limits of model compression and can guide the development of more efficient compression algorithms.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments to validate our theoretical results using various deep learning models and compression algorithms. We will evaluate the compression performance using metrics such as the compression ratio, distortion, and accuracy. We will also compare our results with state-of-the-art compression algorithms to demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk of our approach is that the information-theoretic bounds we derive may not be tight, which could limit their usefulness in practice. Additionally, our approach assumes a specific distortion measure, which may not be suitable for all applications. To mitigate these risks, we will carefully evaluate our results and compare them with empirical methods to ensure that our approach is practical and effective.
